It depends, if the new flush request (i.e. flush_event.set()) happens before the clear, nothing happens as it will be cleared immediatly afterwards. If the new flush request is received after the clear, the event will be set and so in the next loop a new flush happens.
Real concurrency on clear and set is not possible as the `threading.Event` class exactly helps for this case by internally managing a lock so only one operation at a time is possible. In other words, the new request can come only before or after the clear operation. Easy for us :).

Although I'm sympathetic to your concerns about the method in which it abbreviates, sticking with `abbrtask` from Celery simply didn't seem like an option.

The `abbrtask` function from Celery doesn't actually do what the docstring of the `name` function promises. It doesn't cut off at the requested amount of characters. So when I wrote unit tests for the `name` function, I found behavior that contradicts promises made in the docstring.

I had to choose between:

- Rewriting the failing test to accept incorrect behavior
- Sticking with the `abbrtask` function but doing postprocessing on it as a workaround
- Stop using the `abbrtask` function

I went for option 3. Would you make a different choice?

I personally prefer to use explicit lookaheads in such situations:
`[^<]+` would match all characters but not a `<`; `\S` would match `<` too, which makes it less specific: if the expression is too greed we would probably have too much in the `import` group.

For instance when there is a typo (two `<` characters), it wouldn't match correctly:
```python
>>> import re
>>> re.search(r'>(\S+)<', '<foo>bar</foo>').group(1)
'bar'
>>> re.search(r'>(\S+)<', '<foo>bar<</foo>').group(1)
'bar<'
```

although one could argument that then the XML would be invalid and therefore it is okay when the result is wrong.
Just my thoughts :wink:

```python
def _mask_from_lasso_vertices(verts, shape):

    rr, cc = polygon(pr, pc, shape)

    return mask
```

* Get some images of correlated noise in your camera
* Build a CF
* Use this to generate simulations in GalSim with an approximately similar Gaussian noise pattern

`time.time()` isn't guaranteed to be monotonic, e.g. if there is a leap second. Python 3 does provide a monotonic clock

```python
$ python3.5 -c 'import time; print(time.time()); print(time.monotonic())'
1485491671.1016715
569331.546634608
```

but Python 2 does not

```python
$ python2.7 -c 'import time; print(time.time()); print(time.monotonic())'
1485491665.14
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'monotonic'
```

2. that would be exactly it for the conflict case. for transitive dep resolution, the idea would be a `python_binary` that depended on a `python_dist` with a `setup.py` that declared a `install_requires=['some_transitive_dep']` - which would be transitively imported from the `python_binary`'s `main.py` during a `test`/`run`. I believe currently this would fail on the import of `some_transitive_dep` due to no resolver treatment for `setup.py` declared deps.

3. hmm, I don't get the sense it'd be super complex. the first bit would be extracting the injectable requirement spec from the built wheel or `setup.py`, but you can probably get away with simple post-build wheel filename parsing for that (and then e.g. `'=='.join([name, version])`. the second bit would be injecting that inferred spec in with the rest of the transitive requirement specs at build time before they're all passed to the resolver (and ensuring the dir containing the wheel is injected into the `find-links` paths with precedence). afaict, this would solve for both cases described in 2. but a TODO (ideally w/ github issue link) and iteration is fine also.

I really like the idea of a cmd folder with only activate in it, plus the __offer__ of setting up an alias to that activate script.  What I have in mind is:

alias activate "source <full path to activate script>"

Windows would have 3 installation options:
1. Add root env to PATH, as is done now.  python, plus any contained packages will work without additional steps on command line or in scripts.  Leaves room for ambiguity.
2. add proposed cmd folder to path.  Python will not work before activate.bat is called.  Cleaner.  We can also make sure we prepend PYTHONPATH if it is set, and hopefully clear up some newbie issues there.
3. do nothing.  This is still a viable option on windows, because start menu shortcuts to enter an activated environment will be provided.

Hey @davehunt a small comment here: in my experience it is better to try to import using a version check rather than fallback from an import error, because the latter might make it harder to debug some import errors. For example, if one has a botched installation or someone has mocked `configparser` somehow and it fails to import, it will fallback to the second import and "hide" the original error (in Python 2 at least, in Python 3 it won't be a big deal). I had this issue in pytest-dev/pytest-mock#68, where `import mock` was failing because some missing dependencies.

In summary, I suggest using this instead:

```python
if sys.version_info[0] == 2:
    import ConfigParser as configparser
else:
    import configparser
```

Perhaps I don't understand the semantics of the release delay. Here's the scenario we had in mind when we implemented this. If we have `max_sends` objects split into 2 chunks, and the first chunk of each is gotten and released before each second chunk is gotten, then any release delay `<max_sends` (or `<=max_sends` if the bound is inclusive) will result in the first object actually being released. When we get the second chunk of the first object that was gotten, the associated get call to the object store client will incur the usual cost of a get. Perhaps a release delay of `max_sends+1` is sufficient.

It is possible that a high-level framework (e.g. Caffe2, CNTK, or MXNet) implements that Backend API. Internally, the high-level framework can use also use Backend API to leverage hardware-specific backends exposed by vendors. Applications can distinguish between backends using a specific device and backends internally managing execution between multiple devices by doing `onnxGetBackendInfo` for `ONNX_BACKEND_DEVICE_TYPE`. The return value for such high-level backends spanning multiple hardware devices is `ONNX_DEVICE_TYPE_FRAMEWORK`, as opposed to `ONNX_DEVICE_TYPE_NPU`, `ONNX_DEVICE_TYPE_DSP`, `ONNX_DEVICE_TYPE_GPU`, `ONNX_DEVICE_TYPE_CPU`, or `ONNX_DEVICE_TYPE_FPGA` for backends corresponding to real hardware devices.
